stem: According to Fansworth (1957), mental health is best defined as:
options: The absence of any psychiatric disorder or psychopathology
A state of mind in which one is free to make use of his capacities in an effective and satisfying manner
Maladaptive responses to stressors from the internal or external environment
A state of well-being in which the individual realizes his or her own abilities

stem: The World Health Organization (WHO) defines mental health as a state of well-being in which the individual realizes his or her own abilities, can cope with normal stresses of life, and is able to:
options: Avoid all forms of psychiatric disorder
Perform optimally in all situations without any distress
Make a contribution to his or her community
Remain free from all negative emotions

stem: Black and Andreasen (2011) describe mental health as a state of being that is:
options: Absolute and fixed
Relative rather than absolute
Only determinable by psychiatric assessment
Equivalent to the absence of mental illness

stem: Townsend (2015) defined mental health as the successful adaptation to stressors from the internal or external environment, evidenced by thoughts, feelings, and behaviors that are:
options: Completely free of distress and cultural influence
Age-appropriate and congruent with local and cultural norms
Independent of social and occupational functioning
Determined solely by psychiatric diagnosis

stem: Mental health is described as the foundation for which of the following clusters?
options: Physical health, nutrition, and exercise
Emotions, thinking, communication, learning, resilience, hope and self-esteem
Social policy, economic stability, and community services
Medical diagnosis, pharmacological treatment, and clinical assessment

stem: Jahoda (1958) identified how many indicators as a reflection of mental health?
options: Four
Five
Six
Seven

stem: According to Jahoda (1958), 'Autonomy' as an indicator of mental health refers to:
options: The ability to perceive the environment without distortion
The individual's ability to perform in an independent, self-directed manner
The capacity to adaptively respond to the environment
An objective view of self including strengths and limitations

stem: Jahoda's indicator of 'Perception of Reality' includes which of the following?
options: The ability to perform independently without social support
The development of a philosophy of life
Perception of the environment without distortion, and capacity for empathy and social sensitivity
Knowledge and acceptance of one's strengths and limitations

stem: The 'Integration' indicator of mental health according to Jahoda includes the ability to adaptively respond to the environment AND:
options: Seek help from mental health professionals
Avoid all forms of stress
The development of a philosophy of life
Achieve self-actualization at every developmental stage

stem: The Medical Model of normality in mental health defines normal mental health as:
options: Optimal functioning in all areas of life
The absence of any psychiatric disorder or psychopathology
A statistical average within two standard deviations
An absence of distress or help-seeking behaviour

stem: The Statistical Model of normality in mental health defines normal as falling within how many standard deviations of the normal distribution curve?
options: One
Two
Three
Four

stem: The Utopian Model of normality focuses on defining normality as:
options: Absence of psychiatric disorder
Statistical average within the population
Optimal functioning
Absence of distress or disability

stem: According to the Subjective Model of normality, normality is viewed as:
options: Optimal functioning in all life domains
A statistical average for the population
An absence of distress, disability, or any help-seeking behaviour resulting thereof
Conforming to socially acceptable behaviour

stem: The Process Model of normality views normality as:
options: A static and fixed concept
A dynamic and changing process rather than a static concept
The absence of psychiatric disorder
Defined by societal norms only

stem: The Continuum Model of normality considers normality and mental disorder as:
options: Completely separate and unrelated entities
Falling at the two ends of a continuum rather than being disparate entities
Defined solely by psychiatric diagnosis
Equivalent states on a static scale

stem: Which two elements are specifically associated with individuals' perceptions of mental illness, regardless of cultural origin?
options: Stigma and discrimination
Incomprehensibility and cultural relativity
Diagnosis and treatment resistance
Social isolation and occupational impairment

stem: Incomprehensibility in the context of mental illness refers to:
options: The inability to afford mental health treatment
The difficulty in diagnosing mental illness accurately
The inability of the general population to understand the motivation behind the behavior
The complexity of psychiatric terminology

stem: Cultural relativity in mental illness considers that behavior labeled as 'mentally ill' in one society may:
options: Always be considered abnormal globally
Be viewed as normal in another society, and vice versa
Only be assessed by internationally agreed diagnostic criteria
Remain unchanged regardless of cultural context

stem: Townsend (2015) defined mental illness as maladaptive responses to stressors, evidenced by thoughts, feelings, and behaviors that are incongruent with local and cultural norms AND that interfere with:
options: The individual's family planning decisions
Religious and spiritual practices
The individual's social, occupational, and/or physical functioning
Community leadership and civic participation

stem: The American Psychiatric Association defines mental illnesses as health conditions involving changes in:
options: Only neurological structure and brain chemistry
Emotion, thinking or behavior (or a combination of these)
Social relationships and economic status exclusively
Only mood and sleep patterns

stem: A mental disorder is characterized by a clinically significant disturbance in an individual's cognition, emotional regulation, or behaviour. It is usually associated with:
options: Only changes in brain chemistry measurable by scanning
Distress or impairment in important areas of functioning
Complete loss of all mental capacity
Exclusively social withdrawal

stem: Which of the following is listed as an example of mental illness in the document?
options: Hypertension and diabetes
Depression, anxiety disorders, schizophrenia, eating disorders and addictive behaviors
Insomnia, fatigue, and muscle pain
Chronic pain and inflammatory conditions

stem: Which of the following is listed as a characteristic of a mentally-ill person?
options: High self-esteem and strong coping
Consistent mood and emotional stability
Extreme mood changes of highs and lows
Excellent ability to cope with daily problems

stem: According to the Mental HealthâMental Illness Continuum, the absence of mental health:
options: Always means the presence of mental illness
Does not necessarily mean the presence of mental illness
Is equivalent to a diagnosed psychiatric disorder
Cannot occur without severe symptoms

stem: The Continuum Model of Mental Health shows that a person may still have good mental health and wellbeing while:
options: Having no diagnosed conditions whatsoever
Living with a diagnosed mental illness
Showing no symptoms of distress
Meeting criteria for multiple psychiatric disorders simultaneously

stem: A mental health distress becomes a mental illness when:
options: The person refuses to seek professional help
The symptoms last for more than two weeks
Ongoing signs and symptoms cause frequent stress and affect the ability to function
A psychiatric label has been formally assigned

stem: The continuum of mental health spans from which state to which endpoint?
options: Normal functioning to complete psychosis
No distress (healthy) to mental distress, mental health problem, to mental illness (severe symptoms)
Mild anxiety to clinical depression only
Physical health to social dysfunction

stem: Mental health is described as 'completely abstract' because:
options: It can be easily measured with clinical tools
It is only relevant in psychiatric settings
An onlooker does not know what is going on with an individual internally
It changes too rapidly to be studied

stem: The Social Model of normality defines a normal person as one who:
options: Scores within two standard deviations of the population mean
Is free from any psychiatric disorder
Is expected to behave in a socially 'acceptable' behaviour
Functions optimally in all life domains

stem: Mental health implies all of the following EXCEPT:
options: A moderate amount of self-understanding
The capacity to be creative
The ability to love and accept love
Complete freedom from all personal limitations

stem: According to the document, mental health is a component of overall well-being. It can influence and be influenced by:
options: Economic status alone
Physical health
Education and employment only
Social class and political environment

stem: Jahoda's first indicator of mental health â 'A Positive Attitude Toward Self' â includes:
options: The development of a philosophy of life
The ability to perform independently
An objective view of self, including knowledge and acceptance of strengths and limitations
Perception of the environment without distortion

stem: The term 'mental health conditions' is described as a broader term than 'mental disorders' because it also covers:
options: Physical health and neurological disorders only
Psychosocial disabilities and other mental states associated with significant distress, impairment in functioning, or risk of self-harm
Personality traits and learning differences only
Cultural behaviors that differ from societal norms

stem: Being mentally healthy involves having the strength to overcome difficulties and having which of the following?
options: Complete immunity from all life stressors
Unlimited social support networks
Confidence, self-esteem, and the ability to make decisions and believe in oneself
Professional psychiatric certification

stem: When observers are unable to find meaning or comprehensibility in behavior, they are likely to:
options: Seek cultural explanations for the behavior
Label that behavior as mental illness
Provide psychiatric diagnosis based on DSM criteria
Offer social support to the individual

stem: Mental health is a state which is completely _______________ because an onlooker does not know what is going on with an individual, but its manifestations are generally observed in the individual's overt behaviour.
_______________ (1957) defines mental health as a state of mind in which one is free to make use of his capacities in an effective and satisfying manner.
The World Health Organization is abbreviated as _______________.
Black and Andreasen defined mental health in the year _______________.
Townsend (2015) defined mental health as the successful adaptation to _______________ from the internal or external environment.
Mental health involves effective functioning in daily activities resulting in productive activities, healthy relationships, and the ability to adapt to change and cope with _______________.
Mental health is the foundation for emotions, thinking, communication, learning, resilience, hope and _______________.
_______________ (1958) identified a list of six indicators she suggested are a reflection of mental health.

Jahoda's indicator of 'Growth, Development, and the Ability to Achieve Self-actualization' correlates with whether the individual successfully achieves the tasks associated with each level of _______________.
Jahoda's 'Integration' indicator includes the ability to adaptively respond to the environment and the development of a _______________ of life.

Jahoda's indicator of 'Environmental Mastery' suggests that the individual is able to love and accept the love of others, and when faced with life situations is able to strategize, make decisions, change, adjust, and _______________.
The Medical Model of normality is also known as 'Normality as _______________'.
The Statistical Model of normality defines statistically normal mental health as falling within two _______________ deviations of the normal distribution curve for the population.
The Utopian Model is also described as 'Normality as _______________' with its focus on optimal functioning.
The Process Model views normality as a dynamic and changing process rather than as a _______________ concept.
The Process Model of normality can be combined with any _______________ model mentioned.
The two elements associated with individuals' perceptions of mental illness regardless of cultural origin are incomprehensibility and _______________ relativity.
Cultural relativity considers that behavior defined as 'normal' or 'abnormal' is determined by one's _______________ or societal norms.

Mental illness is defined as maladaptive responses to stressors from the internal or external environment, evidenced by thoughts, feelings, and behaviors that are _______________ with the local and cultural norms. (Townsend, 2015)
The _______________ Psychiatric Association defined mental illnesses as health conditions involving changes in emotion, thinking or behavior.

A mental disorder is characterized by a clinically significant disturbance in an individual's cognition, emotional regulation, or behaviour, and is usually associated with distress or _______________ in important areas of functioning.
Mental disorders may also be referred to as mental health _______________.
Examples of mental illness include depression, anxiety disorders, _______________, eating disorders and addictive behaviors.
One characteristic of a mentally-ill person is detachment from _______________.
Mental health and mental illness are influenced by one another but are not _______________ opposites.
In the Continuum Model, it is the _______________ (scores above the 'cutoff') that determines whether a particular person's experience constitutes a symptom of a disorder.
The Continuum Model of Mental Health shows how mental health and mental illness belong to two interconnected but _______________ continua.
A person may have significant problems with their mental health but not meet the _______________ for a diagnosed mental illness.
A mental health distress becomes a mental illness when ongoing signs and symptoms cause frequent stress and affect your ability to _______________.

The continuum of mental health spans from a state of no distress (healthy) to mental distress (mild symptoms), to mental health problem (_______________ symptoms) and then to mental illness (severe symptoms).
Mental health is also key to relationships, personal and emotional well-being and contributing to _______________ or society.
The Social Model of normality expects a normal person to behave in a socially '_______________ ' behaviour.
Jahoda's indicator of 'Perception of Reality' includes perception of the environment without _______________, as well as the capacity for empathy and social sensitivity.
The presence of mental illness does not imply a complete _______________ of mental health.
Mental health has not been easy to define because of several _______________ associated with it.
